section.title: The LORD's Avenging Power
section.sectionRationale: The opening hymn of the book, a tightly woven poem that unveils the character of the God who is about to bring Nineveh down. Before a single Assyrian wall is described as falling, Nahum lifts our eyes to the LORD Himself: jealous, avenging, slow to anger, sovereign over sea and mountain, good to those who trust Him and terrible to His foes.
section.thesis: The God who spared Nineveh in Jonah's day is the same God who is jealous, patient, and just; His avenging power is fearful to His enemies and a refuge to those who take shelter in Him.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the superscription naming the burden; the hymn of the LORD's jealous, avenging majesty; and the twofold verdict, good to those who trust Him, an end to Nineveh.

verses.text: This is the {{massa|burden}} against Nineveh, the book of the vision of Nahum the Elkoshite:

units.label: The jealous and avenging God
units.note: slow to anger, great in power

verses.text: The LORD is a {{qanna|jealous}} and {{naqam|avenging}} God; the LORD is avenging and full of wrath. The LORD takes vengeance on His foes and reserves wrath for His enemies.
The LORD is slow to anger and great in power; the LORD will by no means leave the guilty unpunished. His path is in the whirlwind and storm, and clouds are the dust beneath His feet.
He rebukes the sea and dries it up; He makes all the rivers run dry. Bashan and Carmel wither, and the flower of Lebanon wilts.
The mountains quake before Him, and the hills melt away; the earth trembles at His presence, the world and all its dwellers.
Who can withstand His indignation? Who can endure His burning anger? His wrath is poured out like fire; even rocks are shattered before Him.

units.label: Good to those who trust, an end to His enemies
units.note: a stronghold, and an overwhelming flood

verses.text: The LORD is {{tov|good}}, a {{maoz|stronghold}} in the day of distress; He cares for those who trust in Him.
But with an overwhelming flood He will make an end of Nineveh and pursue His enemies into darkness.

massa.gloss: A burden or oracle, a weighty pronouncement laid upon the prophet. The word can mean a heavy load and a solemn message of judgment at the same time. Nahum opens by naming his book a burden against Nineveh, a divine sentence so heavy it cannot be lifted off or set aside.

qanna.gloss: Jealous, a word used in Scripture only of God. Divine jealousy is not petty envy but the fierce, protective love of a husband for his bride and a king for his honor. Because the LORD is jealous, He will not share His glory with idols or leave the abuse of His covenant people unanswered.

naqam.gloss: To avenge, to take vengeance, to repay wrong with just recompense. Vengeance here belongs to God, who alone judges without malice or excess. Nahum stacks the word three times in a single verse to declare that Nineveh's long cruelty will meet a righteous and certain reckoning.

tov.gloss: Good, kind, generous. In the very hymn that thunders with wrath, Nahum declares that the LORD is good, a refuge for all who trust Him. His goodness and His judgment are not at war; the same God who is a stronghold for His people is the flood that overwhelms their enemies.

maoz.gloss: A place of safety, a fortress or refuge. The word pictures a fortified height where the weak flee for protection. In the day of distress the LORD Himself is this stronghold, and He knows and cares for those who take shelter in Him.

basic.context: Nineveh was the capital of Assyria, the most brutal empire of the ancient world. About a century before Nahum, God had sent Jonah to warn that same city, and it repented and was spared. Now, generations later, Nineveh has returned to its cruelty, and Nahum announces that its time is up. Before describing the fall, chapter one opens with a hymn about the God who brings it.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real prophecy about a real city that really fell, in 612 BC. Nahum is not teaching us to gloat over enemies; he is showing an oppressed people that God sees, God is patient, and God will finally set things right. The tension the chapter holds together is that God is slow to anger and yet by no means clears the guilty.
basic.originalLanguages: A few Hebrew words carry the weight. The book is a burden, massa, a heavy word of judgment. God is jealous, qanna, a word used only of Him, and avenging, naqam, repeated three times in one verse. Yet the same God is good, tov, and a stronghold, maoz, for those who trust Him.
basic.typology: The God who comes in whirlwind and fire, before whom mountains melt, is the God who will one day return in judgment. The flood that makes an end of Nineveh points to the final day when all evil is swept away and the refuge God offers points to the shelter found in Christ.
basic.christ: The wrath Nahum describes is real, and it is good news that God does not wink at evil. At the cross that just wrath fell on Jesus in the place of all who trust Him, so that the God who is a stronghold in the day of distress becomes our hiding place. The good God of Nahum 1:7 is fully revealed in the Son who welcomes the helpless.
basic.god: We learn that God is patient, jealous for His own honor and His people, sovereign over all creation, and perfectly just. His goodness and His severity are two sides of the same holiness. He is a refuge to those who trust Him and an unstoppable flood to those who will not.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 34 gives the words Nahum draws on: the LORD is slow to anger yet will not clear the guilty. Romans 12 tells us to leave vengeance to God. And Nahum 1:7, the LORD is good and knows those who take refuge in Him, is echoed wherever Scripture calls the weak to hide in God.
basic.oneStory: Nahum stands in the long story of a God who is patient with sinners yet finally just with evil. The same holiness that dooms Nineveh is the holiness satisfied at the cross, so that the good God who is a stronghold can gather sinners from every nation safely to Himself.

context.literary: The book opens with a hymn, partly following an acrostic pattern, that functions as an overture to the whole prophecy. It sets the key: this is a book about the character of God before it is a book about the fall of a city. Storm, sea, mountain, and fire imagery portray the LORD as the sovereign warrior whose coming no power can resist.
context.historical: Assyria terrorized the ancient Near East, deporting whole peoples and boasting in its atrocities. It destroyed the northern kingdom of Israel in 722 BC and pressed hard against Judah. Nahum writes after the fall of Thebes in 663 BC, which he mentions in chapter three, and before Nineveh's fall in 612 BC, to a people crushed under Assyrian dominance.
context.canonical: One of the Twelve, Nahum is deliberately the sequel to Jonah. Jonah shows God's mercy to a repentant Nineveh; Nahum shows God's justice on a Nineveh returned to its violence. Together they display the two truths of Exodus 34:6-7, that God is merciful and gracious yet will by no means clear the guilty.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To comfort an oppressed people by revealing the character of the God who is about to judge their oppressor, so that the reader trusts God's justice, takes refuge in His goodness, and stops fearing the powers that seem invincible.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The hymn describes who God is and what He will do to Nineveh; it does not license personal revenge. The abiding truths are the justice, patience, sovereignty, and goodness of God, and the call to take refuge in Him rather than to fear or envy the wicked.

debates.question: How can God be slow to anger and yet by no means clear the guilty, 1:3?

views.label: Two truths held together
views.case: Nahum quotes Exodus 34:6-7, where God's mercy and His justice are stated in the same breath. His patience is real, giving space for repentance as Jonah showed, but patience is not indifference; unrepented evil will finally be judged.

views.label: A tension resolved only at the cross
views.case: Some stress that the deepest answer lies ahead: at Calvary God is shown to be both just and the justifier, punishing sin fully in Christ while sparing the guilty who trust Him, Romans 3:25-26.

debates.bearing: Both readings are true and complementary. God's patience is not weakness and His justice is not cruelty; the two meet perfectly in the gospel, which is why Nahum's wrath is finally good news for those who take refuge in Him.

study.originalLanguages: The overture is built on loaded words. The book is a massa, a burden, a heavy oracle of judgment. God is qanna, jealous, a term reserved for Him alone, expressing His zeal for His honor and His people. He is naqam, avenging, and Nahum repeats the root three times in verse two so the reader cannot miss it. Verse three lifts a line straight from Exodus 34: slow to anger, yet by no means clearing the guilty. Then the hinge of the poem, verse seven: the LORD is tov, good, and a maoz, a stronghold, for those who trust Him, so that the same power that floods Nineveh shelters His own.

typology.type: The LORD coming in whirlwind, storm, and fire
typology.fulfillment: The Lord Jesus revealed from heaven in blazing fire, dealing out justice at His return, 2 Thessalonians 1:7-8.

typology.type: The overwhelming flood that makes an end of God's enemies
typology.fulfillment: The final judgment that sweeps away all evil, prefigured also in the flood of Noah, from which one refuge saved a people.

typology.type: The LORD as a stronghold in the day of distress
typology.fulfillment: Christ as the refuge for sinners, in whom there is no condemnation for those who take shelter in Him, Romans 8:1.

study.christ: Nahum insists that God will by no means clear the guilty, and that is terrifying until we come to the cross. There the jealous, avenging God poured out His just wrath upon His own Son, so that the guilty who trust Him are not swept away in the flood but hidden in the stronghold. The good God of verse seven, who knows and cares for those who take refuge in Him, is seen most clearly in Jesus, who welcomes the helpless and bears the storm in their place. His return will complete what Nahum foresaw, when every enemy of God is finally judged and every refuge-seeker is safe.
study.god: The LORD is jealous for His honor and His people, patient beyond what His enemies deserve, and sovereign over sea, mountain, and storm, so that no power can withstand Him. He is perfectly just: He will not treat evil as though it did not matter. And in the same breath He is good, a stronghold who knows those who trust Him. His goodness and His severity are not in conflict; they flow from one holy character.

crossReferences.note: The source Nahum draws on: the LORD is slow to anger and abounding in love, yet He will by no means clear the guilty.
Nineveh once repented and was spared, showing God's patience; Nahum shows the justice that falls when that patience is spurned.
Vengeance belongs to God, so His people can entrust wrongs to Him rather than avenging themselves.
God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble, the same comfort as Nahum 1:7.

study.oneStory: Nahum belongs to the great biblical account of a God who is patient with sinners yet will not let evil stand forever. The holiness that dooms Nineveh is the very holiness satisfied at the cross, where wrath and mercy meet. So the good God who is a stronghold in the day of distress can gather the helpless from every nation and keep them safe when the flood of judgment finally comes.

sermon.why: No verified expository sermon on Nahum 1:1-8 from a trusted expositor could be confirmed this session; a passage-level exposition can be added later.

quiz.q: How does the opening hymn hold together God's patience and His justice, 1:3?

options.text: He is slow to anger, yet He will by no means leave the guilty unpunished.
options.note: Nahum quotes Exodus 34: real patience and real justice belong to the same God.

options.text: He is patient now but will simply overlook sin in the end.
options.note: His patience is real, but the verse insists the guilty are not left unpunished.

options.text: He is only angry and never patient at all.
options.note: The verse plainly calls Him slow to anger; his patience is genuine.

options.text: His patience means He never judges anyone.
options.note: The whole book announces His just judgment on Nineveh.

quiz.q: What does the Hebrew word qanna, jealous, tell us about God in verse 2?

options.text: It is His fierce, protective zeal for His honor and His people, not petty envy.
options.note: Qanna is used only of God and expresses covenant love that will not share His glory.

options.text: It means God is insecure and easily threatened.
options.note: Divine jealousy is nothing like human insecurity; it is holy, protective love.

options.text: It stirs God's anger against rivals to His glory, though it is far more than mere anger.
options.note: Jealousy does provoke God's anger against idols, but at root it is protective covenant love.

options.text: It shows God is indifferent to how He is treated.
options.note: Jealousy is the opposite of indifference; God cares deeply for His glory.

quiz.q: Right in the middle of a hymn about wrath, what does verse 7 declare?

options.text: The LORD is good, a stronghold in distress, who cares for those who trust Him.
options.note: The same God who floods His enemies is a refuge to those who take shelter in Him.

options.text: The LORD is good only to those who have never sinned.
options.note: The refuge is for those who trust Him, the helpless who take shelter, not the sinless.

options.text: The LORD is good, so no one ever faces His judgment.
options.note: His goodness stands alongside real judgment on His enemies in the very next verse.

options.text: The LORD is distant and cares for no one.
options.note: The verse says He knows and cares for those who trust in Him.

quiz.q: Why is Nahum called the sequel to Jonah?

options.text: Jonah shows God sparing a repentant Nineveh; Nahum shows His justice on a Nineveh returned to its cruelty.
options.note: Together they display God's mercy and His justice, as in Exodus 34:6-7.

options.text: Both concern the same city, Nineveh, though the tie is only that shared setting.
options.note: They do share the city of Nineveh, but the deeper link is the pairing of mercy and justice.

options.text: Nahum repeats Jonah's story word for word.
options.note: Nahum is a distinct prophecy roughly a century later, not a retelling.

options.text: The two books have nothing to do with each other.
options.note: They are deliberately paired around the same city of Nineveh.

quiz.q: How does Nahum's picture of God's wrath become good news in Christ?

options.text: At the cross that just wrath fell on Jesus, so the guilty who trust Him find the stronghold instead of the flood.
options.note: God is shown to be both just and the justifier of those who have faith, Romans 3:26.

options.text: Christ shows that God was never really going to judge sin.
options.note: The cross is God taking sin's judgment with full seriousness, not setting it aside.

options.text: Christ turns God's wrath away from us so that we know His favor.
options.note: Close: Christ does turn the wrath away, but by bearing it Himself, not by it simply vanishing.

options.text: Jesus has no connection to the God of Nahum.
options.note: Jesus reveals and satisfies the very justice and goodness Nahum proclaims.

questions: Nahum shows a God who is patient for a long time but not forever. Where are you tempted to mistake God's patience with evil for His approval of it?
The same chapter calls God avenging and good, a flood to His enemies and a stronghold to His own. How do both truths together shape the way you come to Him?
Verse 7 says God cares for those who take refuge in Him, and taking refuge is simply the act of the helpless. Where do you need to stop running to lesser shelters and hide in God instead?
God tells His people to leave vengeance to Him. Is there a wrong done to you that you are still carrying, that you could entrust to the just Judge?
The wrath Nahum describes fell on Jesus for all who trust Him. How does knowing you are hidden in that stronghold free you from fear of the powers that once seemed unbeatable?

translationNotes.note: Burden, Hebrew massa, can mean both a heavy load and a prophetic oracle; the double sense fits a message of judgment that weighs heavily on the one who bears it and on the city it names.
These verses echo Exodus 34:6-7 closely, deliberately setting God's patience and His justice side by side; Nahum draws the accent onto the justice that Nineveh has spurned.
Where BSB prints an em dash after at His presence, it has been rendered as a comma per house style, with no change to the meaning.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Nahum 1:1-8. Poetic line fragments joined into running verse text; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
